---
title: "Visualization Tools"
---

This section focuses on figures rather than estimators. Its purpose is to show how to build informative, academically formatted visualizations that help readers understand data, summarize model results, and communicate findings clearly. The emphasis is practical: each chapter will walk through one specific figure, explain what it is designed to show, and provide the R code needed to produce it.

The chapters here are meant to complement the statistical, econometric, and mathematical material in the rest of the book. A good visualization is not decoration. It is often one of the fastest ways to detect structure in the data, reveal modeling risks, and translate quantitative results into something a wider audience can actually interpret.

The section begins with a correlation heatmap and then a more general heat-map chapter for visualizing intensity surfaces over two dimensions. It next adds a choropleth-maps chapter for showing regional health variation across geographic areas, then a temporal-series chapter for showing trends, seasonality, and moving-average structure over time, followed by an SEIR trajectory chapter for visualizing latent epidemic dynamics before moving into some of the most useful reporting figures in applied health research: survival curves, calibration plots, calibration belt plots, Bland-Altman plots, forest plots, Love plots for covariate balance, coefficient plots, nomogram plots, variable-importance plots, dot-and-whisker marginal effects plots, ROC and precision-recall curves, DAGs, decision-tree and Markov-model schematics, Sankey diagrams for care pathways, cost-effectiveness planes, and tornado diagrams. Together, these figures cover prognosis, prediction, method agreement, subgroup reporting, causal-design diagnostics, regression interpretation, model reliance, probability-scale summaries, discrimination, individualized risk scoring, causal structure, model structure, epidemic dynamics, longitudinal pattern detection, geographic variation, patient-flow structure, and economic uncertainty, including the practical question of which assumptions matter most in a decision model.
